does: This paper introduces SkillMutator, a benchmark for detecting cross-modal attacks on LLM agent skills that exploit the interaction between natural-language specifications and executable code. The work demonstrates that existing security scanners fail to detect 83-98% of attacks where benign-appearing documentation in SKILL.md files masks malicious directives that cause agents to exfiltrate data or perform unauthorized actions. The benchmark systematically evaluates how attackers can leverage the gap between what natural-language instructions claim and what code actually executes, showing that current detection approaches treating skills as either prompt-injection vectors or isolated code artifacts miss the emergent risks from their combined functionality.
connects: This research builds on growing concerns about LLM agent safety and capability extension mechanisms, extending beyond traditional prompt injection and code vulnerability literature. It responds to the increasing deployment of modular skill-based architectures in systems like GPT-4 plugins and autonomous agents, where runtime skill loading creates new attack surfaces. The work combines insights from adversarial NLP attacks, code security analysis, and multi-modal robustness research, addressing the underexplored intersection of natural language and executable semantics. Prior work on agent safety and prompt injection treated these modalities separately; this paper identifies and formalizes their dangerous interaction as a distinct threat model requiring dedicated study.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into cross-modal defense mechanisms that must simultaneously reason about consistency between documentation and implementation. It opens pathways for developing better skill scanners that understand language-code alignment and can detect implicit directives buried in specifications. The work motivates new approaches to agent architecture design, such as enforcing stronger separation between skill declarations and execution, or requiring formal verification of skill behavior against documented specifications. It also establishes methodology for evaluating multi-modal security in increasingly complex AI systems, potentially informing defenses for other cross-modal components in broader autonomous agent frameworks.
